title: Etale motivic cohomology and algebraic cycles
speaker: Vasudevan Srinivas
tags: Workshop on Chow Groups Motives and Derived Categories

proof_stages.label: Motivic and Lichtenbaum cohomology setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines motivic cohomology by Zariski hypercohomology of Bloch's higher-cycle complex, recalls its identification with higher Chow groups and ordinary Chow groups, and introduces etale motivic/Lichtenbaum cohomology as the same construction in the etale topology.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from integral Chow pathologies
proof_stages.summary: The speaker frames Lichtenbaum Chow groups as a different integral structure on the same rational theory and lists bad integral properties of ordinary Chow groups that the new theory is meant to improve.

proof_stages.label: Deligne-Beilinson targets and Bloch's cycle-map machine
proof_stages.summary: Deligne-Beilinson cohomology, Abel-Jacobi maps, regulators, and Bloch's axiomatic construction of generalized cycle maps from higher Chow groups to suitable cohomology theories are recalled as the comparison framework.

proof_stages.label: Etale-descent factorization theorem
proof_stages.summary: The first main result states that any Bloch-type cohomology theory satisfying etale descent receives its Bloch cycle map through etale motivic cohomology, yielding a commutative factorization diagram applicable to Beilinson and Deligne-Beilinson cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Torsion and divisibility theorem over separably closed fields
proof_stages.summary: For a smooth projective variety over a separably closed field and l prime to the characteristic, the Lichtenbaum motivic l-primary torsion is identified with etale cohomology with Q_l/Z_l coefficients, and the corresponding Q_l/Z_l quotient vanishes in the stated range.

proof_stages.label: Proof via finite-coefficient comparison and weights
proof_stages.summary: The torsion theorem is proved by universal-coefficient diagrams, the finite-coefficient motivic-to-etale isomorphism, and a weight argument showing that the rational Lichtenbaum-to-l-adic map is zero, forcing the desired torsion isomorphism and divisibility.

proof_stages.label: Complex-geometric consequences
proof_stages.summary: Specializing to complex varieties and Deligne-Beilinson cohomology gives torsion isomorphisms, torsion-free cokernels, divisibility of Lichtenbaum Griffiths groups, a description of the kernel from ordinary to Lichtenbaum Chow groups via Abel-Jacobi torsion, and an integral reformulation of the Hodge conjecture for Lichtenbaum cycles.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic non-finite-generation application
proof_stages.summary: As an arithmetic application, a CM elliptic curve self-product over a localized integer ring with an ordinary prime inverted is shown to have Lichtenbaum CH^2 containing Q_p/Z_p, explained heuristically by a nontrivial residue along the omitted good ordinary fiber.

examples.name: Pathological integral Chow phenomena over C
examples.note: The existence of smooth projective varieties with infinite mod-l Chow groups, infinite l-torsion, failures of the integral Hodge conjecture, and noninjective specialization is used to motivate the change from ordinary Chow groups to Lichtenbaum Chow groups.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker lists pathological Chow-group phenomena over C as known existence results, without constructing or computing any specific example

passes.reason: Smooth projective varieties with infinite mod-l/torsion Chow behavior and integral Hodge or specialization failures are used to refute hoped-for integral properties, not as structurally exceptional objects.
These smooth projective complex varieties are invoked to show integral Chow finiteness, torsion-control, integral Hodge, and specialization-injectivity expectations fail.

smooth projective complex varieties with infinite Chow mod-l or torsion phenomena are used to refute naive integral finiteness, divisibility, Hodge, and specialization expectations, with no special intrinsic structure specified

These smooth projective complex varieties are invoked to refute integral finiteness, torsion, specialization, and integral Hodge expectations, with no intrinsic extremal, symmetric, exceptional, or degenerate structure specified.

Smooth projective complex varieties with infinite mod-l Chow quotients, infinite torsion, integral Hodge failures, or bad specialization are used as failures of expected integral finiteness/control properties, but are not sporadic, symmetric, degenerate, or extremal objects.

The smooth projective complex varieties with infinite mod-l or torsion Chow behavior are cited to show expected integral finiteness, Hodge, and specialization properties fail, not because they are sporadic or structurally extreme.
Used to exhibit failures of integral finiteness, torsion control, integral Hodge, and specialization for Chow groups, with pathology in all possible nonclassical codimensions and all primes.

Smooth projective complex varieties with pathological integral Chow behavior are used to refute desired finiteness, torsion, integral-Hodge, and specialization properties, without being sporadic, symmetric, degenerate, or formally extremal.
Smooth projective complex varieties are cited to refute integral finiteness/Hodge/specialization properties, with maximal all-prime/all-relevant-degree pathology.
Used as maximally pathological smooth projective complex varieties whose integral Chow groups have infinite mod-l or torsion behavior, refuting hoped-for finiteness/integral properties.

used to exhibit failures of finiteness, integral Hodge, and specialization properties for ordinary Chow groups, with no indicated intrinsic symmetry or classification-exception structure in the varieties.

Used to exhibit failures of expected integral finiteness, torsion, integral Hodge, and specialization properties of ordinary Chow groups; the varieties are not structurally sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Bloch's torsion zero-cycle argument
examples.note: Bloch's earlier treatment of torsion zero-cycles is cited as the precedent from which the weight-vanishing step in the l-adic comparison argument is adapted.
examples.engagement_reason: Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle argument is cited as an earlier precedent for the weight argument, but the zero-cycle case itself is not carried out

passes.reason: Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle case concerns top-codimension zero-cycles, a boundary case, and is cited as a precedent for the weight argument rather than to disprove a claim.
The transcript cites Bloch’s proof/weight argument rather than a specific mathematical object whose structure can be classified.
Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle case is the dimension-zero endpoint of cycle theory and is cited as a precedent for the weight/torsion argument, not as a refutation
Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle argument is cited as a precedent for the weight/Galois-cohomological proof method rather than as an object disproving a claim.

Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle case is cited as a prior template for the weight/torsion argument, and zero-cycles form the zero-dimensional boundary case among algebraic cycles rather than a counterexample.
Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle case concerns the dimension-zero boundary case of algebraic cycles and is used as a precedent for the weight argument rather than to refute a claim.
Cited as a precedent for the weight-vanishing argument, concerning zero-cycles, the minimal-dimensional boundary case of algebraic cycles.
Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle case is cited as a prior supporting weight argument, and zero-cycles form the minimal-dimensional boundary case of algebraic cycles.
Bloch’s torsion zero-cycle result is used as a positive model for the weight argument, and zero-cycles are the boundary/minimal-dimensional case of algebraic cycles.
Cited as a precedent for the weight argument, with zero-cycles forming the top-codimension boundary case of algebraic cycles.
cited as a precedent for the weight/torsion argument rather than to refute a claim, and zero-cycles are a boundary-dimensional case among algebraic cycles.
Cited as a prior proof technique rather than as a disproof, with zero-cycles forming the dimension-zero endpoint among algebraic cycles.

examples.name: CH^2 and unramified cohomology comparison sequence
examples.note: A short exact sequence involving codimension-two cycles and unramified cohomology illustrates how the Zariski-to-etale comparison spectral sequence packages failures of integral Hodge-type statements.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker invokes the CH^2 comparison exact sequence, says it is produced by the comparison spectral sequence, and uses it to relate integral Hodge failure to unramified cohomology

passes.reason: The CH^2–unramified cohomology exact sequence is a standard comparison tool relating integral Hodge defects to unramified cohomology, not a special object or counterexample itself.
The CH^2–unramified cohomology short exact sequence is used as a standard comparison explaining a relation, not as an adversarial example.
the codimension-two Chow/unramified-cohomology exact sequence is used as a standard comparison explaining the integral Hodge defect, not as an adversarial example
